articles.title: Todd Blanche, Attorney General? — See Also
articles.content:
"我觉得他会，是的"：特朗普认为他前刑事辩护律师"做得很好"。

"解释是给下级法院的"：最高法院拒绝为阿拉巴马州使用种族歧视选区地图提供理由。

"我很忙，去跟我*说"：大律所推出人工智能合伙人供律师助理互动。

"最大中的最大"：美国500家最大律所排名！

"本周《像律师一样思考》节目"：法官办公室恋情和人工智能调查！

这篇《Todd Blanche，司法部长？——参见》文章首发于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
“I Think He Will, Yeah”: Trump thinks his former criminal defense attorney is doing a “very good job.”

Explaining Is For Lower Courts: SCOTUS opts out of justifying Alabama’s use of racist election maps.

I’m Busy, Go Talk To Me*: Biglaw firm launches AI partners for associates to interact with.

The Biggest Of The Big: These are the country’s 500 largest law firms!

This Week On Thinking Like A Lawyer: Judicial chambers hook ups and AI sleuthing!

The post Todd Blanche, Attorney General? — See Also appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: The Real Difference Between The Highest And Lowest Paid Biglaw Equity Partners
articles.content:
编辑注：欢迎来到我们的每日专栏“每日 trivia 问答”！

根据 Law.com 最近对大型律师事务所的调查，2025年Am Law 100中最高薪酬合伙人与最低薪酬合伙人之间的平均差距是多少？

提示：这些数字反映了大型律师事务所“合伙人人才自由球员时代”日益增强的灵活性。

<下一页查看答案>

本文首发于 Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Ed. Note: Welcome to our daily feature Trivia Question of the Day!

According to a recent Law.com survey on Biglaw firms, what was the average distance between the highest- to lowest-paid partners in the Am Law 100 in 2025?

Hint: The numbers reflect the increased flexibility in Biglaw’s “free-agent era for law firm partner talent.”

See the answer on the next page.

The post The Real Difference Between The Highest And Lowest Paid Biglaw Equity Partners appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Why Fewer People Are Voting For Judges And Why That Might Be A Good Thing
articles.content:
昨天，加州举行了州级和地方选举。大多数媒体关注的是州长职位的初选以及洛杉矶市长的竞选。但过去几天，几位朋友一直在问我应该投票给哪位司法候选人。对于不了解情况的人来说，加州的初审法院法官由县居民选举产生，任期六年。

大多数人认为，要么我认识这些候选人，能获得他们的内部背景信息，要么仅凭我的经验就能给出推荐。虽然我认识其中几位，但了解得还不够深入，无法做出推荐。对于其他人选，我和朋友们一样一无所知。

我决定研究一位我之前从未听说过的法官候选人，我称他为候选人X。我在Ballotpedia.org等网站上查看他的背景信息。信息很基础，但至少候选人X没有谷歌搜索留下的负面信息。

然后我去了几个律师论坛，询问关于候选人X的信息，以及这个人是否具备担任法官的资格。有些人给出了好评，但其他人通过私信发送了一些看似可信的负面信息。

我对候选人X的对手进行了类似的研究，我称他为候选人Y。结果相似：网上没有不良记录，一些同事公开称赞他，而少数对候选人Y评价不高的人给我发了私信。我还应该指出，一位朋友对候选人Y的推荐实际上让我产生了怀疑，因为这位朋友和我在当今95%的问题上都礼貌地持不同意见。所以并非所有背书都是等价的。

因此，对于询问候选人X和Y情况的人，我告诉他们我的发现，询问他们在法官身上看重哪些品质，然后根据他们的回答给出建议。

虽然对候选人X和Y的上述研究没有占用太多时间，但我无法对其他候选人做同样的事情。所以对于其他人选，我建议他们上网搜索候选人姓名，查看律师协会网站了解他们的推荐。之后，可以根据研究结果投票。如果他们懒得做这些，可以去他们选择的AI网站，输入他们认为法官应具备的重要品质，看看AI支持谁。

几乎所有与我交谈过的人都表示，他们会直接放弃投票，因为他们没有足够的信息来进行明智的投票。

如果有些人因为缺乏信息或不愿意对候选人进行基本的网络搜索而不投票给司法候选人，那么可以安全地假设，大多数投票的人都做了充分的研究，或者收到了可信赖的人的推荐。少数人可能基于其他标准投票，比如相似的种族或民族背景。我对候选人X和Y的研究大约花了一小时。

也许较低的投票率是件好事。如果一位法官是通过争议性选举和高投票率当选的，那么他或她可能会觉得自己有隐性授权，以特定方式裁决分歧问题。

如前所述，我认识的大多数人之前从未听说过这些司法候选人。我没有看到任何候选人在电视或广播上竞选，大多数人也没有大规模的互联网或社交媒体存在感。所以这可能意味着司法候选人要么没有足够的钱进行全面的竞选活动，要么这种花钱方式不值得。

此外，我认为美国人总体上不习惯投票选举法官。由于联邦法院经常出现在新闻中，人们可能会误以为州法官也是由州长任命并由议会批准的。此外，一些选民可能不理解法官的工作是公正地适用法律来解决争端，即使这意味着结果不是他们希望看到的。法官不应该成为一个超级立法机构。

在司法选举中投票的人较少是有充分理由的：大多数选民缺乏关于候选人的充分信息。但这种低投票率实际上可能是有益的。这可能意味着那些投票的人更加知情，或者依赖可信的推荐。这是否有助于通过保护法官免受高投票率、高党派色彩选举带来的强烈政治压力来维护司法独立性？法官应该是法律的公正仲裁者，而不是寻求取悦多数人的政客。

 Steven Chung是加州洛杉矶的一位税务律师。他帮助人们进行基本的税务规划并解决税务纠纷。他也对背负大量学生贷款的人表示同情。他可以通过电子邮件stevenchungatl@gmail.com联系他。您也可以在Twitter(@stevenchung)上关注他，并在LinkedIn上与他联系。

本文《为什么越来越少的人投票给法官，而这可能是件好事》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Yesterday, California held its state and local elections. Most of the media focus was on the primaries for the governor’s seat and the mayoral race in Los Angeles. But over the past few days, several friends have been asking me about which judicial candidates they should vote for. For those who don’t know, trial court judges in California are elected by the county residents and serve six-year terms.

Most of them thought that either I knew the candidates personally, had inside access to their background, or could give a recommendation solely based on my experience. While I know a few, I don’t know them well enough to make a recommendation. And I am just as clueless as they are on the rest.

I decided to research one judge candidate, whom I will call Candidate X, that I have never heard of before. I looked at their background information on places like ballotpedia.org. The information was basic but at least Candidate X didn’t have any Google gremlins.

I then went to a few lawyer message boards and asked for information about Candidate X and whether this person is qualified to be a judge. Some responded with praise but others sent me private messages with less than stellar information that seemed credible.

I did similar research on Candidate X’s opponent, whom I will call Candidate Y. The result was similar: no bad internet footprint, some colleagues publicly praised him while a few who didn’t think highly of Candidate Y sent me direct messages. I should also note that a friend’s recommendation of Candidate Y actually made me skeptical because this friend and I politely disagree on 95%b of the issues of the day. So not all endorsements are created equal.

So for anyone who asked about Candidates X and Y, I told them my findings, asked a few questions about what qualities they are looking for in a judge, and then gave them a recommendation based on their answers.

While doing the above research on Candidates X and Y didn’t take up too much of my time, I couldn’t do the same for other candidates. So for the rest, I suggested that they look up the candidate’s name on the internet, check bar association websites to see which they recommend. Afterward, they can vote based on their research results. If they are feeling lazy, they can go to the AI website of their choice, input the qualities important to them in a judge, and see who the AI endorses.

Almost everyone I talked to ended up saying that they would just leave the ballot blank since they do not have enough information to vote intelligently.

If some people are not voting for judicial candidates because of lack of information or because they cannot bring themselves to even do a basic internet research on the candidate, then it is safe to assume that most of those who do vote have done adequate research or received a recommendation from someone they trust. A few may vote based on other criteria such as similar racial or ethnic backgrounds. My research on Candidates X and Y took about an hour.

Perhaps a smaller voter turnout can be a good thing. If a judge were to be elected through a contentious election with high voter turnout, then the judge may feel that he or she has an implicit mandate to rule a certain way on divisive issues.

As mentioned earlier, most people I know never heard of the judicial candidates before. I don’t see any candidate campaigning on television or radio and most don’t have a large internet or social media presence. So it probably means that judicial candidates either do not have the money for a full-scale election campaign or it is not cost-effective to spend money that way.

Also, I think Americans in general are not used to voting for judges. Since federal courts tend to be in the news more often, people might mistakenly think state judges are also appointed by the governor and approved by the legislature. Also, some voters may not understand that a judge’s job is to apply the law impartially to resolve disputes, even if it means the outcome is not what they wanted to see. Judges are not supposed to be a super-legislature.

Fewer people vote in judicial elections for good reason: most voters lack sufficient information about the candidates. But this low turnout may actually be beneficial. It likely means that those who do vote are more informed or are relying on trusted recommendations. Could this help preserve judicial independence by shielding judges from the intense political pressures that come with high-turnout, highly partisan races? Judges should be impartial arbiters of the law, not politicians seeking to please the majority.

Steven Chung is a tax attorney in Los Angeles, California. He helps people with basic tax planning and resolve tax disputes. He is also sympathetic to people with large student loans. He can be reached via email at stevenchungatl@gmail.com. Or you can connect with him on Twitter (@stevenchung) and connect with him on LinkedIn.

The post Why Fewer People Are Voting For Judges And Why That Might Be A Good Thing appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Biglaw Firm Launches AI Partner Doppelgangers To Help Train Associates
articles.content:
上周，斯蒂芬·恩布里写了一篇文章宣称，过去的模式——在饮水机旁偶遇高级合伙人，从他们随口抛出的建议中学习如何成为一名优秀律师——已经彻底消亡。新冠疫情夺走了任何人是否真的在办公室工作的确定性。强制返岗令和大律所疯狂购买房地产的热潮正迫使人们重新回到彼此身边，但这并不意味着那种社群关系就自然而然回来了。律师们没有离开座位去向资深律师请教，而是开始使用律所提供的内置大语言模型来帮助简化工作流程。意识到这一点——以及其他种种问题——比如过度依赖大语言模型会减少初级律师与人类同事之间的信任，恩布里鼓励合伙人们改变培训方式：

[我们必须努力用更正式、经过设计的培训来替代非正式的饮水机交流，创造初级律师之间以及初级律师与合伙人之间互动的机会。结构化的培训确保培训真正发生，确保每个人都能获得培训，而不仅仅是那些恰好在正确时间出现在饮水机旁的幸运儿。我们必须摒弃对计费工时的短视专注，腾出非计费时间来创造所需的培训和互动。

这听起来很棒，但要是我们直接把这事交给人工智能呢？

沃里斯、萨特、西摩和皮斯律师事务所推出了一种让初级律师与合伙人紧密合作的新方式。路透社对此进行了报道：

沃里斯律所开发了19位合伙人的“人工智能人设”，可以嵌入生成式人工智能工具中，以个别合伙人的风格回答问题并修改文件。

这些人设本质上就像一个人工智能聊天机器人，用合伙人的思维方式生成回复和修改文件。这些数字人格是通过数小时的访谈开发出来的，涵盖律师执业方法、价值观和经验。他们不是基于合伙人的工作成果进行训练，而是试图体现他们的个人思维方式。

“这几乎就像拥有了一张低分辨率的、大脑的地图，”沃里斯律所软件、数据和创新高级总监内特·杰迪纳克说道。

啊，是的。经典操作：“我派分身去应付，自己去做真正重要的工作”：

不管怎样，初级律师和合伙人都觉得这些人工智能合伙人皮肤很有用。一位合伙人表示，他很惊讶人工智能回答问题的方式听起来很像他。现在我们只需要斯坦福大学进行一项双盲研究，来确定人工智能合伙人是否比他们各自的生物原型更好地教导初级律师。

这项创新并没有被宣传为合伙人专业知识的替代品。但它仍然有非常大的用武之地。对于较小的事项，通过人工智能来运行问题可能比咨询每小时收费数千美元的合伙人更划算。对于更大的问题，门显然还是敞开的——人工智能合伙人的反馈与真正合伙人想法之间的任何差异，都可能揭示出推理过程中的细微差别，从而创造绝佳的学习机会。

在大型律所采用人工智能的故事中，我很有兴趣看到这个律所和采用这项技术的其他律所最终会如何。

律师们，见见你们的人工智能“分身”[路透社]

早前报道：ChatGPT是否刚刚取代了法学院办公时间？

是时候向饮水机培训说再见了

克里斯·威廉姆斯于2021年6月成为Above the Law的社交媒体经理和副主编。在加入该团队之前，他在Facebook群组“法学院段子手的边缘T14 meme”中兼职担任一个次要的梗王。他在密苏里州熬到从圣路易斯华盛顿大学法学院毕业。他曾是一名造船工人，正在学习游泳，对修辞学、斯宾诺莎主义者和幽默感兴趣。重新开始骑自行车也不会有什么坏处。你可以通过电子邮件christopherrashadwilliams@gmail.com或Tweet/Bluesky @WritesForRent联系他。

本文首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Last week, Stephen Embry wrote an article declaring that the old model of running into a senior partner at the water cooler and picking up their dropped gems about how to be a good lawyer is dead and gone. COVID took away the certainty that anyone was even working in the office. Office mandates and Biglaw real estate buying sprees are forcing people back in proximity with each other, but that doesn’t mean that the community is there. Rather than leaving the desk to get advice from senior attorneys, associates have been turning to the in-house LLMs firms have been providing to help streamline workflows. Recognizing that, among other things, heavy reliance on LLMs can reduce the trust associates have with their human colleagues, Embry encouraged partners to change how they approach training:

[W]e have to work to replace the informal water cooler with more formal and designed training that creates the opportunities for interaction between associates and between associates and partners. Structured training ensures that training will actually happen and that all will get the training, not just those who are lucky enough to be at the water cooler at the right time. We have to set aside the myopic focus on billable hours and commit to nonbillable time to create the training and the interactions that are needed.

That’s cool and all, but what if we just delegated that to AI?

Vorys, Sater, Seymour and Pease launched a new way for associates to work closely with their partners.* Reuters has coverage:

Vorys has developed “AI personas” of ​19 of its partners, which can be embedded within generative AI tools to offer responses to questions and edit documents in the style of individual partners.
…
The personas essentially work as an AI chatbot that spits out replies and edits documents in the mindset of the partner. The digital personalities are developed from hours-long interviews about the attorney’s ​approach to practicing law, values and experiences. They aren’t trained on work product from the partners, but instead seek to embody their individual thinking styles.

“It’s almost like having a low resolution map of the person’s brain,” said Nate Jedinak, Vorys’ senior director ​of software, data and innovation.

Ah, yes. The ol’ “I’ll phone it in with my clones while I’m doing the actual, important work” gambit:

For what it’s worth, both associates and partners have found the AI partner skins useful. One partner said that he was surprised that the AI’s responses to questions sounded a lot like him. Now all we need is for Stanford to run a double blind study to determine if the AI partners do a better job of teaching associates than their respective biological inspirations.

The innovation isn’t being sold as a replacement for partner expertise. But there is still room for it to be very useful. It will probably be more cost effective to run questions about smaller matters through the AI than to ask a several-thousand-an-hour partner what they think about things. And for bigger problems, the door will presumably still be open — any differences in opinion between the AI partner feedback and what the actual partner thinks about the issue could tease out nuances in reasoning that make for great learning opportunities.

As far as Biglaw AI adoption stories go, I’m interested in seeing how this pans out for the firm and firms that adopt the technology.

Lawyers, Meet Your AI ‘Twin’ [Reuters]

Earlier: Did Chat GPT Just Replace Law School Office Hours?

It’s Time To Say Goodbye To Water Cooler Training

Chris Williams became a social media manager and assistant editor for Above the Law in June 2021. Prior to joining the staff, he moonlighted as a minor Memelord™ in the Facebook group Law School Memes for Edgy T14s .  He endured Missouri long enough to graduate from Washington University in St. Louis School of Law. He is a former boat builder who is learning to swim and is interested in rhetoric, Spinozists and humor. Getting back in to cycling wouldn’t hurt either. You can reach him by email at christopherrashadwilliams@gmail.com and by Tweet/Bluesky at @WritesForRent.

The post Biglaw Firm Launches AI Partner Doppelgangers To Help Train Associates appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: 20 Nonfiction Book Recommendations To Jumpstart Your Summer Reading Season
articles.content:
每年我都会写一篇专栏，推荐一些我认为值得阅读的非虚构类书籍。以前我会列出当年读过的前15本，后来因为无法精简，变成了前20本。现在我打算一读到20本真正值得推荐的非虚构类作品就发布列表。

一如既往，我会在文末附上历年书单的链接，以防你需要更多选择。虽然为了方便起见，我会为很多书提供亚马逊链接，但我强烈建议你如果可以的话，去当地独立书店购买这些书。闲话少叙，以下是我推荐的20本非虚构类书籍，值得你在今夏湖边度假、休假或随时阅读。

《灌木丛中的恶魔：瑟古德·马歇尔、格罗夫兰男孩们与美国新时代的黎明》——吉尔伯特·金

明尼苏达州共和党刚刚为被定罪的杀人犯德里克·肖文举行了一场不合时宜的默哀，但即便这样的恶魔，与这个故事核心那个腐败、种族主义的警察相比，看起来也像只泰迪熊了。另一方面，瑟古德·马歇尔和他那些高尚的同僚们或许能让你重拾对人性和律师行业的信心。

《荒野的呼唤：穿越美国亚文化之旅》——路易斯·塞鲁克斯

说到白人至上主义，纪录片导演路易斯·塞鲁克斯在他的第一本书中对此有相当深入的探讨。塞鲁克斯目前在Netflix上有一部相当火热的影片关于男权圈层，你可以从这本书中获得他早期作品的一些有益洞见。

《他们希望我成为的那个人：有毒的男子气概与我们时代的一场危机》——贾里德·耶茨·塞克斯顿

我们这里不会从传统的海滩读物开始。但话说回来，回家时带着晒伤的皮肤和更好的理解如何对抗有毒的男子气概，不是比仅仅晒伤更有意义吗？

《一场精彩的对决：三个非凡的人、即将到来的世界大战，以及史上最伟大的一场网球比赛》——马歇尔·乔恩·费舍尔

嘿，网球，这算是夏季运动了。纳粹则不那么讨人喜欢。不幸的是，纳粹在这个故事中扮演了重要角色，主要体现在他们如何渗透到二战前生活的各个方面，包括规定谁可以或不可以参加体育运动。这听起来是不是有些耳熟？

《对决：美国西部最伟大的枪战》——詹姆斯·雷森纳

现在这本是你在啜饮皮纳科拉达时真的可以轻松翻阅的。我不知道为什么关于旧西部牛仔互相残杀的故事读起来如此轻松。有些事情就是如此。

《乌鸦杀手：肝脏猎人约翰逊的传奇》——雷蒙德·W·索普 Jr. 和罗伯特·班克

部分是历史人物，部分是民间英雄，全部都是硬汉：肝脏猎人约翰逊肯定不会消除任何挥之不去的有毒男子气概。正如你所料，约翰逊的故事中有些部分相当种族主义。然而，你仍然会感到惊讶，而且我敢说会被他更宏大的宽恕弧线所感动。

《大草原之海：美洲大平原的征服、毁灭与救赎》——戴夫·哈格和约瑟芬·马科蒂

大草原长期以来一直是一片被忽视的景观。作者们尝试并成功地将美国曾经伟大的大草原以其应得的庄严呈现出来。

《白色深渊》——大卫·格兰

虽然大草原曾经被错误地描绘成荒凉之地，但南极几乎无法用其他方式描述。这本关于南极痴迷者的轻快冒险传记既令人兴奋又令人动容。

《明尼唐卡湖畔》——埃里克·德雷格尼

双城最大湖泊的含义远不止钓鱼和划船。王子已经明白了这一点：你也应该明白。

《大明尼苏达：探索蓝天碧水之地》——帕特里克·希克斯

在这份书单中再加入另一本明尼苏达主题的书——对我来说这是艰难的一年。我们值得沉浸在一点州立自豪感中。这本书将带你远超双城。

《纽约的101件物品史》——萨姆·罗伯茨

今年的地区历史，我不会遗漏大苹果。我在上西区的旧货店买了我的副本，它和它所在的商店一样古怪。

《复活鲨鱼：科学痴迷与解开2.7亿年前化石之谜的特立独行者》——苏珊·尤因

这个关于揭示一块石头中保存的神秘牙齿漩涡的故事，每一处都像侦探小说一样引人入胜。世界是一个奇怪的地方，它之前也曾经是许多奇怪的地方。让自己沉浸在其中一个吧。

《朗姆酒事件：植物学欺诈的真实故事》——卡尔·萨巴克

这不是字面上的朗姆酒，但如果你愿意的话，可以回到前几段的皮纳科拉达。你会得到一个鲜明的提醒：永远不要相信任何声称自己品格好就必然在所有事情上都诚实的人。

《牛顿的暴政：斯蒂芬·格雷和约翰·弗拉姆斯蒂德被压制的科学发现》——戴维·H·克拉克和斯蒂芬·H·P·克拉克

还要记住，一个人在一个知识领域出色，并不意味着那个人在其他领域也是正派的，或者在该领域的专业知识方面有所了解。艾萨克·牛顿是个混蛋。虽然他在物理学方面取得的进步对他的时代来说是惊人的，但他仍然凭借自己纯粹的混蛋行为，至少在电学技术的发展上造成了倒退。想知道是怎么回事吗？读这本书吧。

《一张非常欠揍的脸：回忆录》——科林·乔斯特

我对回忆录很挑剔。幸运的是，读完他的书后，我想揍科林·乔斯特的冲动减少了。

《孙子兵法》——孙子

我仍然想揍乔斯特最近在《周六夜现场》中扮演的内阁成员，主要是因为他似乎没有读过这本书或任何其他关于成功指挥战争的东西。这些教训中有多少在今天仍然适用，这真的很了不起，以及特朗普政府在伊朗战争中几乎没有应用其中任何一个。

《教会与仇恨的分离：理智者从原教旨主义者、法西斯主义者和敛财骗子手中夺回圣经的指南》——约翰·富格尔桑

和约翰·富格尔桑一样，我真的很不想读这本书，因为我早就知道，大多数认为自己是的基督徒的人甚至不尝试遵循耶稣基督的实际教导。这是一本重要的著作，在直接引用圣经方面非常有用，以证明"保守派基督徒"这个词本身就是矛盾修饰法。太糟糕了，最需要读这本书的人没有一个会读它。

《利奥波德国王的幽灵：殖民非洲的贪婪、恐怖和英雄主义》——亚当·霍赫schild

这是一本关于殖民主义的绝佳论证。一个多世纪后，利奥波德的纯粹邪恶仍然令人震惊。

《森林绅士：男子气概、神话和美国伐木工人》——威拉·哈米特·布朗

啊，过去的伐木工——那个被误解的人。研究细致，文笔引人入胜，这首北方森林的歌谣揭开了错位的浪漫主义，直接触及了往往严酷的现实。

《天堂之地：一个真实故事，讲述最后的边疆和育空淘金热》——霍华德·布隆

一位平克顿侦探、一位探矿者和一个只有最后一次机会大赚一笔的失败骗子，在一系列传奇冒险的过程中相遇。这是一个乐趣。

享受这个夏天吧。如果需要更多非虚构类书籍推荐，请点击以下链接：

《20本非虚构类书籍推荐，大部分由女性撰写，适合节日礼物和未来一年》

《15本最佳非虚构类书籍，可供自己阅读或作为礼物赠送，均非回忆录或政治类》

《15本最佳大众兴趣非虚构类书籍，你不容忽视的2024年阅读清单》

《15本最适合添加到2023年及以后阅读清单的大众兴趣非虚构类书籍》

《15本在疫情第二年阅读的优秀大众兴趣非虚构类书籍》

《15本在每个人最糟糕的一年阅读的最佳大众兴趣非虚构类书籍》

乔纳森·沃尔夫是一位民事诉讼律师，著有《你的无债务法学博士》。他教授法律写作，为各种刊物撰稿，并将财务和科学素养作为自己的业务和乐趣。他表达的任何观点可能都是纯金，但仍然仅代表他自己，不应归因于任何他所属的组织。他也不想分享荣誉。可以通过jon_wolf@hotmail.com联系他。

这篇文章《20本非虚构类书籍推荐，助你开启夏季阅读季》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Every year I commit a column to describing a few nonfiction books that I deem worth reading. It used to be my top 15 I’d read that calendar year, then, as I failed to narrow it down, my top 20. Now I’m simply going to put out a list as soon as I get to 20 heartily worthwhile nonfiction reads.

As always, I’ll include links to lists from previous years at the end in case you need even more options, and even though I’ll link a lot to Amazon for ease of use on many of these titles, I’d encourage you to go to your local independent bookstore if you can to actually purchase any of these. With that, in no particular order, here are 20 nonfiction books I can recommend as worth your time this summer at the lake, on vacation, or nowhere in particular.

Devil in the Grove: Thurgood Marshall, the Groveland Boys, and the Dawn of a New America by Gilbert King

The Minnesota GOP just held an ill-conceived moment of silence for convicted murderer Derek Chauvin, but even a monster like him almost looks like a Teddy bear compared to the crooked, racist cop at the center of this story. On the other hand, Thurgood Marshall and his noble colleagues might just restore your faith in humanity, not to mention lawyers.

The Call of the Weird: Travels in American Subcultures by Louis Theroux

Speaking of white supremacy, documentarian Louis Theroux focuses pretty heavily on it in this, his first book. Theroux has a pretty hot film out on Netflix right now about the manosphere, and here you can get some useful insight into his earlier work.

The Man They Wanted Me to Be: Toxic Masculinity and a Crisis of Our Own Making by Jared Yates Sexton

We’re not starting out with traditional beach reads here. Still, wouldn’t you rather come home from the beach with a sunburn and a better understanding of how to combat toxic masculinity than only a sunburn?

A Terrible Splendor: Three Extraordinary Men, a World Poised for War, and the Greatest Tennis Match Ever Played by Marshall Jon Fisher

Hey, tennis, that’s kind of summery. Nazis, less so. Unfortunately, Nazis play a prominent role in this drama, mainly in how they infected every area of life prior to WWII, including dictating who could and couldn’t play sports. That sound at all familiar?

Draw: The Greatest Gunfights of the American West by James Reasoner

Now here’s one that you really can breeze through while sipping a piña colada. I don’t know what it is about cowboys murdering each other in the Old West that makes for light reading. Some things just are the way they are.

Crow Killer: The Saga of Liver-Eating Johnson by Raymond W. Thorp Jr. and Robert Bunker

Part historical figure, part folk hero, all man: Liver-Eating Johnson is certainly not going to dispel any lingering toxic masculinity. As you can imagine, parts of Johnson’s saga are pretty racist. However, you will nonetheless be surprised, and, dare I say touched, at his broader arc of forgiveness.

Sea of Grass: The Conquest, Ruin, and Redemption of Nature on the American Prairie by Dave Hage and Josephine Marcotty

The prairie has long been a neglected landscape. The authors try, and succeed, in portraying America’s once great prairies with the majesty they deserve.

The White Darkness by David Grann

While prairies were once wrongly portrayed as desolate, Antarctic can hardly be described in any other way. This snappy little adventure biography of an Antarctica obsessive is both exciting and moving.

By the Waters of Minnetonka by Eric Dregni

There is a lot more to the premier lake of the Twin Cities than a little fishing and boating. Prince had it figured out: so should you.

Greater Minnesota: Exploring the Land of Sky-Blue Waters by Patrick Hicks

Indulge me with another Minnesota-themed title on this list — it’s been a tough year for Minnesotans like me. We deserve to wallow in a little state pride. This one will take you well beyond the Twin Cities.

A History of New York in 101 Objects by Sam Roberts

I won’t leave out the Big Apple in my regional histories this year. I picked up my copy at a thrift shop on the Upper West Side, and it proved as idiosyncratic as the store it came from.

Resurrecting the Shark: A Scientific Obsession and the Mavericks Who Solved the Mystery of a 270-Million-Year-Old Fossil by Susan Ewing

This tale of getting to the bottom of a mysterious swirl of teeth preserved in stone is every bit as arresting as a detective novel. The world is a strange place, and it has been many strange places before. Submerge yourself in one of them.

A Rum Affair: A True Story Of Botanical Fraud by Karl Sabbagh

It’s not literally about rum, but feel free to go back to that piña colada from a few paragraphs ago if you’d like. You’ll get a stark reminder never to trust anyone who claims their supposed good character means they are necessarily honest in all things.

Newton’s Tyranny: The Suppressed Scientific Discoveries of Stephen Gray and John Flamsteed by David H. Clark and Stephen H. P. Clark

Also remember that a person being brilliant in one area of knowledge does not make that person decent or indicate that said person knows anything at all when it comes to other areas of expertise. Sir Isaac Newton was a dick. Although the advancements he made in physics were astounding for his time, he still managed to singlehandedly set our progress on electrical technologies back by at least a couple decades through his pure, unadulterated dickishness. Find out how within.

A Very Punchable Face: A Memoir by Colin Jost

I am very picky about memoirs. Fortunately I wanted to punch Colin Jost less by the time I finished his.

The Art of War by Sun Tzu

I do still want to punch the cabinet member Colin Jost has been portraying on SNL lately, mainly because he seemingly hasn’t read this or anything else about successfully orchestrating a war. It’s really amazing how many of these lessons remain relevant today, and how little anyone in the Trump administration applied any of them in the war with Iran.

Separation of Church and Hate: A Sane Person’s Guide to Taking Back the Bible from Fundamentalists, Fascists, and Flock-Fleecing Frauds by John Fugelsang

As much as I like John Fugelsang, I really didn’t want to read this book, because I already knew that the majority of people who believe themselves to be Christians do not even attempt to follow the actual teachings of Jesus Christ. It’s an important work, though, and is really useful in going right to the Bible to demonstrate how the phrase “conservative Christian” is an oxymoron. Too bad none of the people who need to read it the most will.

King Leopold’s Ghost: A Story of Greed, Terror and Heroism in Colonial Africa by Adam Hochschild

This is about as good an argument as you’re going to find against colonialism. Leopold’s sheer depravity shocks the conscience more than a century hence.

Gentlemen of the Woods: Manhood, Myth, and the American Lumberjack by Willa Hammitt Brown

Ah, the misunderstood lumberjack of yore. Meticulously researched, enticingly readable, this ballad of the Northwoods peels aside the misplaced romanticism to get right on down to the often harsh reality.

The Floor of Heaven: A True Tale of the Last Frontier and the Yukon Gold Rush by Howard Blum

A Pinkerton, a prospector, and a washed up grifter with one last chance to score big cross paths during the course of a series of grand adventures. It’s a delight.

Enjoy the summer. Here are the links to previous lists if you need even more nonfiction book recommendations:

20 Nonfiction Book Recommendations, Most Written By Women, For Holiday Gifts And The Year Ahead

15 Best Nonfiction Books To Read Yourself Or Give As Gifts, None Of Them Memoirs Or About Politics

15 Best General-Interest Nonfiction Books You Dare Not Overlook For Your 2024 Reading List

15 Of The Best General-Interest Nonfiction Books To Add To Your Reading List In 2023 And Beyond

15 More Excellent General-Interest Nonfiction Books Read During The Second Year Of The Pandemic

15 Best General-Interest Nonfiction Books Read During The Worst Year Of Everyone’s Life

Jonathan Wolf is a civil litigator and author of Your Debt-Free JD (affiliate link). He has taught legal writing, written for a wide variety of publications, and made it both his business and his pleasure to be financially and scientifically literate. Any views he expresses are probably pure gold, but are nonetheless solely his own and should not be attributed to any organization with which he is affiliated. He wouldn’t want to share the credit anyway. He can be reached at jon_wolf@hotmail.com.

The post 20 Nonfiction Book Recommendations To Jumpstart Your Summer Reading Season appeared first on Above the Law.
